jokes.text: Now, that sign says "applesauce." No, no, no, I'm kidding. It says "applause."
jokes.setup: Pointing to a cue card sign, saying it says something absurd
jokes.punchline: Correcting to the obvious truth that it says 'applause'
jokes.explanation: Basic misdirection joke for audience warm-up. Gets the job done but extremely predictable.

dialogue.speaker: Warm-up comedian
dialogue.line: Now, that sign says "applesauce." No, no, no, I'm kidding. It says "applause."

jokes.text: And now, because he's already been paid, so we might as well use him... Larry Sanders!
jokes.setup: Standard glowing introduction format for a talk show host
jokes.punchline: Undercutting it with mercenary reasoning about payment
jokes.explanation: Perfect Hank character moment - his tendency to undercut moments with inappropriate honesty. Well-crafted subversion of intro format.

dialogue.line: And now, because he's already been paid, so we might as well use him... Larry Sanders!

jokes.text: you're so much better than last night's crowd
jokes.setup: Thanking the audience in what should be a complimentary way
jokes.punchline: Backhanded compliment that insults the previous audience
jokes.explanation: Standard talk show host material - mild subversion of audience flattery but not particularly inventive.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: you're so much better than last night's crowd

jokes.text: Dan Quayle has gotten confused and just thinks there should be more Tru-Value Hardware stores
jokes.setup: George Bush talking about family values as campaign issue
jokes.punchline: Dan Quayle mishearing 'family values' as 'Tru-Value' hardware stores
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay that works on multiple levels - mishearing and corporate reference. Good craft for political humor.

dialogue.line: Dan Quayle has gotten confused and just thinks there should be more Tru-Value Hardware stores

jokes.text: they're still giving him a picture menu and letting him point at the food he wants
jokes.setup: Dan Quayle can't spell 'potato' but you have to blame White House staff
jokes.punchline: They treat him like a child with picture menus
jokes.explanation: Strong escalation from the spelling gaffe to infantilizing imagery. Visual and memorable.

dialogue.line: they're still giving him a picture menu and letting him point at the food he wants

jokes.text: He has a new song coming out, however, called "Die, Crossing Guard, Die."
jokes.setup: Ice-T pulled controversial 'Cop Killer' song
jokes.punchline: Even more ridiculous target for violence - crossing guards
jokes.explanation: Absurdist escalation works well - crossing guards are harmless authority figures, making the threat comically disproportionate.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: He has a new song coming out, however, called "Die, Crossing Guard, Die."

jokes.text: If I was a rapper, I already have my name picked out. It would be "Hey, Put Ice on That."
jokes.setup: All rappers have 'ice' in their names
jokes.punchline: Larry's would be about literal ice for injuries
jokes.explanation: Excellent character-specific humor - Larry as middle-aged Jewish guy completely missing the point of rap names. The injury reference is perfect for his neurotic persona.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: If I was a rapper, I already have my name picked out. It would be "Hey, Put Ice on That."

jokes.text: I know I would hurt myself, and my big controversial song would be "Hey, Mom, Get Off My Back."
jokes.setup: Larry's rapper persona with injury-themed name
jokes.punchline: His 'controversial' song is about maternal nagging
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation that perfectly captures Larry's neurotic, suburban mindset. The contrast between gangsta rap rebellion and Jewish mother issues is expertly executed.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I know I would hurt myself, and my big controversial song would be "Hey, Mom, Get Off My Back."

jokes.text: Hank the Rapper
jokes.setup: Larry asks what Hank's rap name would be
jokes.punchline: Completely literal, unimaginative name
jokes.explanation: Perfect Hank moment - his complete lack of imagination and cultural awareness delivered with complete confidence.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: What would your rap name be?

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Hank the Rapper

jokes.text: What the fuck does Dana Carvey know about doing this show?
jokes.setup: Larry just announced Dana Carvey as guest host on air
jokes.punchline: Hank immediately complains off-air about the choice
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Hank's jealousy and lack of professionalism. The transition from on-air to off-air creates the cringe.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: What the fuck does Dana Carvey know about doing this show?

jokes.text: He does funny voices. That's what he does. "I'm going to give you a pump up."
jokes.setup: Hank dismissing Dana Carvey's qualifications
jokes.punchline: Bad impression of Hans and Franz while criticizing impressions
jokes.explanation: Meta humor about SNL impressions while Hank attempts and fails at one himself. Good irony in the execution.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: He does funny voices. That's what he does. "I'm going to give you a pump up."

jokes.text: I outgrew that horseshit in high school
jokes.setup: Hank criticizing Dana's sketch comedy background
jokes.punchline: Claims he outgrew it while being incredibly immature himself
jokes.explanation: Perfect irony - Hank claiming maturity while displaying childish jealousy. Classic character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: I outgrew that horseshit in high school

jokes.text: Not really. Hee Haw was on.
jokes.setup: Larry asks if Artie watched the show
jokes.punchline: Admits he watched lowbrow variety show instead
jokes.explanation: Classic Artie - brutal honesty delivered matter-of-factly. The choice of Hee Haw is perfectly lowbrow.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Not really. Hee Haw was on.

jokes.text: I'm just warming up. I'm doing your show, I gotta do you.
jokes.setup: Larry asks what Dana is doing
jokes.punchline: Dana thinks he needs to impersonate Larry to host
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Dana's SNL mindset of doing impressions for everything.

dialogue.speaker: Dana
dialogue.line: I'm just warming up. I'm doing your show, I gotta do you.

jokes.text: You can't believe what it's doing in Malaysia. It's through the roof. - It's so sad.
jokes.setup: Larry bragging about his movie's success in Malaysia
jokes.punchline: Dana sarcastically calling it 'so sad' while Larry agrees
jokes.explanation: Good interplay showing Larry's obliviousness to sarcasm and Dana's dry wit.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: You can't believe what it's doing in Malaysia. It's through the roof.

dialogue.speaker: Dana
dialogue.line: It's so sad.

jokes.text: The one sleeping bag thing didn't work. - First time I heard a complaint.
jokes.setup: Larry mentions bringing your own sleeping bag
jokes.punchline: Dana implies sharing a sleeping bag was problematic
jokes.explanation: Mild cringe humor about awkward camping arrangements. Larry's oblivious response adds to the discomfort.

dialogue.speaker: Dana
dialogue.line: The one sleeping bag thing didn't work.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: First time I heard a complaint.

jokes.text: That's very interesting, Marty, but can you do this with your card?
jokes.setup: Hank practicing talk show hosting with writer
jokes.punchline: Treating a normal conversation like a magic trick reveal
jokes.explanation: Great character moment showing Hank's complete misunderstanding of talk show hosting dynamics.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: That's very interesting, Marty, but can you do this with your card?

jokes.text: My next guest is a good friend and a great talent. Come on, put your hands together and give a big welcome for Phil... the writer.
jokes.setup: Hank doing standard guest introduction format
jokes.punchline: Introducing a random writer as if he's a celebrity
jokes.explanation: Excellent cringe comedy - Hank's desperate practice session with inappropriate fanfare for a non-celebrity.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: My next guest is a good friend and a great talent. Come on, put your hands together and give a big welcome for Phil... the writer.

jokes.text: Don't mention the name Rupert Pupkin.
jokes.setup: Artie warning about Hank's fragile ego
jokes.punchline: Reference to 'King of Comedy' character who's a delusional wannabe host
jokes.explanation: Brilliant reference that perfectly captures Hank's delusional aspirations. Economy of a perfect cultural touchstone.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Don't mention the name Rupert Pupkin.

jokes.text: Schwing.
jokes.setup: Hank asks if it's him or Dana Carvey
jokes.punchline: Artie does Dana's catchphrase as his answer
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective - Artie's brutal efficiency in dismissing Hank using Dana's own catchphrase.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Schwing.

jokes.text: She's the one who reminds me I can't say "cocksucker" when I'm a guest. - Now you're the host, so feel free. - Cocksucker.
jokes.setup: Paula mentions censoring Dana's language as a guest
jokes.punchline: Immediately uses the forbidden word when told he can as host
jokes.explanation: Simple escalation joke about newfound freedom. Dana's immediate compliance is childishly funny.

dialogue.speaker: Paula
dialogue.line: She's the one who reminds me I can't say "cocksucker" when I'm a guest.

dialogue.speaker: Dana
dialogue.line: Now you're the host, so feel free.

dialogue.speaker: Paula
dialogue.line: Cocksucker.

jokes.text: NBC can eat shit and die.
jokes.setup: Barbara politely asking Dana not to mention NBC
jokes.punchline: Immediately followed by vicious attack on the network
jokes.explanation: Sharp tonal shift from polite request to vicious corporate hatred. Good character moment for network executive.

dialogue.speaker: Barbara
dialogue.line: NBC can eat shit and die.

jokes.text: Oh, yeah. We sent a videotape, but nonetheless...
jokes.setup: Dana asks if they went to Gulf War to entertain troops
jokes.punchline: Hank admits they just sent a tape but acts like it was heroic
jokes.explanation: Perfect Hank moment - taking credit for military service when they barely did anything. The 'nonetheless' is perfectly delusional.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Oh, yeah. We sent a videotape, but nonetheless...

jokes.text: before that I was a cruise director... so, all told, that gives me 12 years experience getting people to interact.
jokes.setup: Hank explaining his hosting experience
jokes.punchline: Counting cruise director work as equivalent to talk show hosting
jokes.explanation: Great character logic - Hank's inflated sense of his qualifications. Cruise directing as media training is perfectly absurd.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: before that I was a cruise director... so, all told, that gives me 12 years experience getting people to interact.

jokes.text: listening.
jokes.setup: Dana asks for hosting tips
jokes.punchline: Hank delivers one-word profound wisdom with dramatic pause
jokes.explanation: Brilliant deadpan moment. Hank's dramatic delivery of obvious advice as if it's profound wisdom.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: listening.

jokes.text: Does that help you at all? Or should I just go fuck myself?
jokes.setup: Hank gives vague advice about listening
jokes.punchline: Immediately becomes defensive and self-deprecating
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Hank's insecurity masked as aggression when his advice isn't immediately praised.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Does that help you at all? Or should I just go fuck myself?

jokes.text: you just give me a glance, I'm gonna jump right in... because that's how I carry half the show.
jokes.setup: Hank offering to help Dana if he gets in trouble
jokes.punchline: Claims he carries half of Larry's show
jokes.explanation: Perfect Hank delusion - vastly overestimating his contribution while offering unsolicited help.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: you just give me a glance, I'm gonna jump right in... because that's how I carry half the show.

jokes.text: I'm in trouble. - I got you. Hey, now.
jokes.setup: Practicing the emergency glance system
jokes.punchline: Hank's 'rescue' is just his catchphrase, which doesn't help
jokes.explanation: Great payoff to the glance setup - Hank's 'help' is completely useless, just his ego-driven catchphrase.

dialogue.speaker: Dana
dialogue.line: I'm in trouble.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: I got you. Hey, now.

jokes.text: That snotty little shit. He's not even gonna do the glance. I'll bet he's like that twit, Leno.
jokes.setup: Hank alone after trying to help Dana
jokes.punchline: Immediately turns bitter and insulting about both Dana and Leno
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Hank's petty jealousy and tendency to lash out when rejected.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: That snotty little shit. He's not even gonna do the glance. I'll bet he's like that twit, Leno.

jokes.text: I'm gonna leave him standing there with his tallywhacker right in his hand.
jokes.setup: Hank threatening not to help Dana
jokes.punchline: Crude metaphor for leaving him helpless
jokes.explanation: Typical Hank - crude language to express petty revenge. 'Tallywhacker' is appropriately juvenile for his character.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: I'm gonna leave him standing there with his tallywhacker right in his hand.

jokes.text: I'm not worthy.
jokes.setup: Dana starting his hosting monologue
jokes.punchline: Uses his Wayne's World catchphrase
jokes.explanation: Simple callback to Dana's SNL character. Functional but not particularly inventive.

dialogue.speaker: Dana
dialogue.line: I'm not worthy.

jokes.text: I feel funny... like when I used to climb the rope in gym class.
jokes.setup: Dana describing his nervousness
jokes.punchline: Specific gym class reference that implies physical arousal
jokes.explanation: Good specific reference that works on multiple levels - nervousness and subtle sexual humor.

dialogue.speaker: Dana
dialogue.line: I feel funny... like when I used to climb the rope in gym class.

jokes.text: Schwing.
jokes.setup: Following the gym class rope reference
jokes.punchline: His Wayne's World catchphrase for arousal
jokes.explanation: Predictable use of catchphrase, but works as follow-up to the gym class reference.

dialogue.speaker: Dana
dialogue.line: Schwing.

jokes.text: You better not undo your belt. You might cause a "flab-alanche."
jokes.setup: Dana doing Hans character commenting on audience member
jokes.punchline: Made-up word combining 'flab' and 'avalanche'
jokes.explanation: Solid wordplay in character. 'Flab-alanche' is inventive and fits Hans's mean-spirited workout character.

dialogue.speaker: Dana
dialogue.line: You better not undo your belt. You might cause a "flab-alanche."

jokes.text: Sorry, I had a little Satan twister. Sorry.
jokes.setup: Dana appearing to lose control during monologue
jokes.punchline: Blaming it on a 'Satan twister'
jokes.explanation: 'Satan twister' is an inventive absurd excuse for losing control. Good random humor.

dialogue.speaker: Dana
dialogue.line: Sorry, I had a little Satan twister. Sorry.

jokes.text: Well, well, well, so many sluts and whores, oh, my.
jokes.setup: Dana switching to Church Lady character
jokes.punchline: Church Lady's judgmental assessment of the audience
jokes.explanation: Standard Church Lady material - judgmental religious character calling out perceived immorality.

dialogue.speaker: Dana as Church Lady
dialogue.line: Well, well, well, so many sluts and whores, oh, my.

jokes.text: You know, come on, anyone can do a monologue. Byron Allen does a monologue.
jokes.setup: Backstage reaction to Dana's successful monologue
jokes.punchline: Dismissing it by comparing to notoriously bland Byron Allen
jokes.explanation: Perfect Hank jealousy - dismissing Dana's success with the most damning comparison possible in late-night TV.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: You know, come on, anyone can do a monologue. Byron Allen does a monologue.

jokes.text: I always feel like I look like I should be in the window at Sears. "All boys' wear, half off."
jokes.setup: Dana asking Herve about growing a beard
jokes.punchline: Self-deprecating comparison to a children's clothing mannequin
jokes.explanation: Excellent self-deprecating humor with very specific, visual imagery. The 'half off' adds extra punch to the boyish appearance joke.

dialogue.speaker: Dana
dialogue.line: I always feel like I look like I should be in the window at Sears. "All boys' wear, half off."

jokes.text: He made Herve Villechaize seem funny, but without making fun of him.
jokes.setup: Watching Dana interview Herve
jokes.punchline: Artie's professional assessment of Dana's interviewing skill
jokes.explanation: Good professional observation about the delicate balance of interviewing a difficult guest with dignity.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: He made Herve Villechaize seem funny, but without making fun of him.

jokes.text: Pretty insane, but she thought you were Danish Carver.
jokes.setup: Fan meeting Dana after the show
jokes.punchline: Mispronouncing his name as 'Danish Carver'
jokes.explanation: Simple name mispronunciation joke. 'Danish Carver' is mildly amusing but not particularly clever.

dialogue.speaker: Fan
dialogue.line: Pretty insane, but she thought you were Danish Carver.

jokes.text: I threw up in it. - Oh, where? - In your top desk drawer. - Yeah, that's what that's for.
jokes.setup: Dana thanking Larry for the office
jokes.punchline: Casual admission of vomiting in the desk, Larry treating it as normal
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurd escalation with perfect deadpan response. Larry treating vomit drawer as normal office feature is inspired.

dialogue.speaker: Dana
dialogue.line: I threw up in it.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Oh, where?

dialogue.speaker: Dana
dialogue.line: In your top desk drawer.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Yeah, that's what that's for.

jokes.text: Hey, you clods. Who died? Hey, I'm just kidding. If someone did die, I was making a joke. Sorry.
jokes.setup: Larry entering room of serious-looking people
jokes.punchline: Misreading the room then awkwardly over-explaining his joke
jokes.explanation: Good cringe comedy - Larry's social awkwardness and tendency to dig himself deeper with explanations.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Hey, you clods. Who died? Hey, I'm just kidding. If someone did die, I was making a joke. Sorry.

jokes.text: After one night?
jokes.setup: Told CBS offered Dana a talk show
jokes.punchline: Larry's stunned reaction to the timeline
jokes.explanation: Good reaction beat showing Larry's disbelief at how quickly the business moves.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: After one night?

jokes.text: If I said something every time I saw something coming... we'd never get anything done.
jokes.setup: Larry asking why Artie didn't warn him about CBS offer
jokes.punchline: Artie's cynical explanation of his management philosophy
jokes.explanation: Perfect Artie logic - brutal honesty about his fatalistic approach to show business. Great character consistency.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: If I said something every time I saw something coming... we'd never get anything done.

jokes.text: Who... Who moved my desk? What is this? - Mr. Carvey said there was a draft in this corner. - A draft?
jokes.setup: Larry notices his desk was moved
jokes.punchline: Dana's prima donna behavior about a draft
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing Dana's immediately entitled behavior as guest host.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Who... Who moved my desk? What is this?

dialogue.speaker: Assistant
dialogue.line: Mr. Carvey said there was a draft in this corner.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: A draft?

jokes.text: Thanks, Mannix.
jokes.setup: Artie doing investigative legwork on Dana's offer
jokes.punchline: Reference to 1970s detective show
jokes.explanation: Good cultural reference that fits Larry's generation and mocks Artie's investigative approach.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Thanks, Mannix.

jokes.text: You know... Boy, I do not... Yeah, there's a little something.
jokes.setup: Larry checking for the draft Dana complained about
jokes.punchline: Reluctantly admitting there might actually be a draft
jokes.explanation: Good physical callback - Larry's grudging admission undermines his indignation about the desk move.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: You know... Boy, I do not... Yeah, there's a little something.

jokes.text: Heard the good news. Congratulations. - Thanks, yeah. - That is great. I was gonna tell you in the hallway earlier, but I didn't get a chance.
jokes.setup: Hank congratulating Dana on his success
jokes.punchline: Fake enthusiasm masking his jealousy and resentment
jokes.explanation: Good cringe moment showing Hank's inability to be genuinely happy for others. The false enthusiasm is perfectly uncomfortable.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Heard the good news. Congratulations.

dialogue.speaker: Dana
dialogue.line: Thanks, yeah.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: That is great. I was gonna tell you in the hallway earlier, but I didn't get a chance.

jokes.text: God knows I'm not friends with Arsenio... and I swear to God, Leno tried to hit me with one of his big cars the other day.
jokes.setup: Larry talking about not wanting competition to ruin friendship
jokes.punchline: Escalating to absurd claim about Leno's vehicular assault
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation from reasonable concern to paranoid absurdity. 'One of his big cars' is a perfect detail that makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: God knows I'm not friends with Arsenio... and I swear to God, Leno tried to hit me with one of his big cars the other day.

jokes.text: Schwing.
jokes.setup: Awkward moment as Larry and Dana part ways
jokes.punchline: Dana's catchphrase as response to awkward situation
jokes.explanation: Another catchphrase usage, this time as awkward exit strategy. Less effective than earlier uses.

dialogue.speaker: Dana
dialogue.line: Schwing.

jokes.text: Some of the things you do are not quite right.
jokes.setup: Hank asking for specific criticism after being told he wasn't quite right
jokes.punchline: Artie's barely more specific non-answer
jokes.explanation: Perfect Artie moment - giving Hank exactly what he asked for while being completely unhelpful. Great deadpan delivery.

dialogue.line: Some of the things you do are not quite right.

jokes.text: Hank, you speak very slowly.
jokes.setup: Hank demanding specific criticism
jokes.punchline: Artie pointing out Hank's painfully obvious speech pattern
jokes.explanation: Good observational humor - stating the obvious about Hank's delivery in a way that's both helpful and brutal.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Hank, you speak very slowly.

jokes.text: After the show, remind me to turn you on to a little thing called decaf.
jokes.setup: Hank being hyperactive during interview
jokes.punchline: Dana's professional but condescending suggestion
jokes.explanation: Good professional putdown delivered as helpful advice. Shows Dana learning to manage difficult sidekicks.

dialogue.speaker: Dana
dialogue.line: After the show, remind me to turn you on to a little thing called decaf.

jokes.text: I'm hosting the show tonight. What? That's right. I'm hosting the show tonight. I was bored on vacation anyway.
jokes.setup: Larry suddenly announcing his return
jokes.punchline: His casual dismissal of his vacation
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Larry's impulsiveness and need for control.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I'm hosting the show tonight. What? That's right. I'm hosting the show tonight. I was bored on vacation anyway.

jokes.text: You can't say? Oh, Artie, he can't say. - No, he can't say. - He can't say.
jokes.setup: Dana saying he can't reveal his decision
jokes.punchline: Three-way repetition building frustration
jokes.explanation: Good escalation through repetition. Shows mounting frustration at Dana's evasiveness.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: You can't say? Oh, Artie, he can't say.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: No, he can't say.

dialogue.speaker: Barbara
dialogue.line: He can't say.

jokes.text: I'm taking the deal at NBC.
jokes.setup: Dana says he's not taking the CBS deal
jokes.punchline: Reveals he's taking a different, worse deal
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection - the relief of him turning down CBS immediately destroyed by worse news about NBC.

dialogue.speaker: Dana
dialogue.line: I'm taking the deal at NBC.

jokes.text: I'm not taking the deal with CBS. I apologize, sir. Thank you. Why? I'm taking the deal at NBC.
jokes.setup: Dana appearing to decline CBS offer
jokes.punchline: The double twist - he has an even better NBC deal
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection comedy with perfect structure. The double twist lands perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: Dana
dialogue.line: I'm not taking the deal with CBS. I apologize, sir. Thank you.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Why?

dialogue.speaker: Dana
dialogue.line: I'm taking the deal at NBC.

jokes.text: I didn't even know about the NBC thing. I saw it coming. I hate this business. I'm telling you, I saw it coming. And I'm telling you, I hate this fucking business.
jokes.setup: Larry's shock at NBC news
jokes.punchline: The escalating argument about who predicted what
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing the characters' different coping mechanisms under stress.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I didn't even know about the NBC thing.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: I saw it coming.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I hate this business.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: I'm telling you, I saw it coming.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: And I'm telling you, I hate this fucking business.

jokes.text: I saw it coming. - I hate this business. I'm telling you, I saw it coming. And I'm telling you, I hate this fucking business.
jokes.setup: Larry expressing frustration
jokes.punchline: Artie's continued claims of prescience vs Larry's rage
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing character differences - Artie's fatalism vs Larry's emotional investment.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: I saw it coming.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I hate this business. I'm telling you, I saw it coming. And I'm telling you, I hate this fucking business.

jokes.text: Is she with lips or without lips? - I wanted to talk to you about that. One of her lips is thin, and the other one's still fat.
jokes.setup: Larry asking about Barbara Hershey's appearance
jokes.punchline: Artie's detailed description of her cosmetic surgery results
jokes.explanation: Good observational humor about celebrity plastic surgery. The specific detail about uneven lips is perfectly absurd.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Is she with lips or without lips?

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: I wanted to talk to you about that. One of her lips is thin, and the other one's still fat.

jokes.text: Yeah, I got another fishing trip offer. Bob Saget called. Huge fishing offer. It's multifaceted.
jokes.setup: Larry asking if Dana has other fishing trip offers
jokes.punchline: Treating Bob Saget fishing trip like a business deal
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist escalation - treating a casual fishing trip as a 'multifaceted' business opportunity. Perfect show business parody.

dialogue.speaker: Dana
dialogue.line: Yeah, I got another fishing trip offer. Bob Saget called. Huge fishing offer. It's multifaceted.

jokes.text: You're talking to us like we're Martin and Lewis, for God's sakes.
jokes.setup: Dana asking if they patched things up
jokes.punchline: Larry's comparison to famous comedy duo breakup
jokes.explanation: Good reference to famous comedy partnership dissolution. Shows Larry's awareness of show business history.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: You're talking to us like we're Martin and Lewis, for God's sakes.

jokes.text: Just wanted to let you know, it's prime time. It would be hours before you'd ever be on television. - So don't feel threatened. - I wasn't threatened.
jokes.setup: Dana trying to reassure Larry about the NBC deal
jokes.punchline: Making it worse by pointing out Larry's supposed fear
jokes.explanation: Good cringe moment - Dana's attempt at reassurance only makes Larry more defensive.

dialogue.speaker: Dana
dialogue.line: Just wanted to let you know, it's prime time. It would be hours before you'd ever be on television. - So don't feel threatened.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I wasn't threatened.

jokes.text: Well, it could be late night.
jokes.setup: Dana claiming his NBC show is prime time
jokes.punchline: Final revelation that it might directly compete with Larry
